uniprot_keywords: Host cell receptor for virus entry

targetability.tldr: HSPA1A/HSP70 conditionally localizes to the plasma membrane outer leaflet of tumor and stress-exposed cells via lipid anchoring, absent from normal cell surfaces. Preclinical programs (cmHsp70.1 mAb, TKD peptide-NK cell approach) have validated membrane HSP70 as an oncology target.

extracellular_domain.notes: When peripherally surface-anchored, the full protein is extracellular. C-terminal SBD epitope confirmed accessible by cmHsp70.1 mAb binding to intact viable cells.

induced_presentation.description: Surface-exposed in many tumor types (colorectal, lung, neuronal, pancreas carcinomas, leukemia) but absent from normal tissues. ~15-20% of total Hsp70 localizes to the plasma membrane of Hsp70-positive tumor sublines. Anchored via Gb3 glycosphingolipid in cholesterol-rich microdomains.

Heat shock triggers phosphatidylserine (PS) increase, driving HSPA1A translocation to the plasma membrane. Inhibiting PS synthesis with fendiline or RNAi blocks membrane localization. Distinct from the basal oncogenic surface pool.
ICD inducers (NIR-PIT, anthracyclines) trigger surface exposure of HSP70 alongside calreticulin and HSP90, serving as damage-associated molecular patterns that stimulate anti-tumor immunity.

surface_localization_assays.cell_type_or_line: freshly isolated primary tumor biopsies (colorectal, lung, neuronal, pancreas carcinomas; AML leukemic blasts)
CX+ colon carcinoma and Colo+ pancreatic carcinoma tumor sublines
heat-shocked cancer cells (confocal microscopy with PS-specific biosensors)
ovarian cancer cells (NIR-PIT immunogenic cell death model)

preclinical_evidence.finding_summary: Surface biotinylation of intact viable CX+ and Colo+ tumor cells showed ~20% of total Hsp70 on the plasma membrane. Flow cytometry with cmHsp70.1 mAb (targeting an extracellularly exposed C-terminal SBD epitope) confirmed 73-87% of Hsp70-positive tumor subline cells were surface-positive; normal cells had <3%. Depletion of Gb3 glycosphingolipid with PPMP1 eliminated membrane Hsp70, confirming lipid-anchored extracellular accessibility on intact viable cells.

risk_flags.description: HSPA1A is actively released into the extracellular space via a non-classical lysosomal-endosomal pathway (UniProt: Secreted). Elevated circulating eHSP70 is documented in cancer, sepsis, and exercise/heat-stress states. This soluble pool may compete with extracellular binders targeting membrane-anchored mHSP70, reducing effective surface engagement.

isoform_accessibility.rationale: Canonical 641 aa isoform. All primary surface expression data (flow cytometry, surface biotinylation, immunofluorescence) refers to this form. No differential surface localization documented for isoform 2.
No isoform-specific subcellular annotation in UniProt and no literature evidence for differential surface localization relative to the canonical form.

evidence.claim: UniProt annotates HSPA1A as Secreted in addition to its primary cytoplasmic and nuclear locations, documenting an extracellular released form.

confidence_reasoning: All 8 M1 database sources vote false — HSPA1A lacks TM domain, signal peptide, and GPI anchor, so canonical surface-prediction pipelines correctly reject it. However, primary experimental literature (surface biotinylation on intact viable tumor cells, flow cytometry with cmHsp70.1 mAb on non-permeabilized cells, and immunofluorescence confocal microscopy) robustly establishes conditional outer-leaflet localization in transformed cells and under heat/ICD stress. Normal cells are consistently surface-negative. The mechanism (peripheral lipid anchoring via Gb3 glycosphingolipid and phosphatidylserine in cholesterol-rich microdomains, non-classical secretory pathway) is well characterized. The conditional nature (strictly absent from normal cells), non-canonical membrane anchor, and substantial circulating soluble pool each reduce confidence relative to a constitutively surface-expressed protein with a canonical TM helix. Medium confidence is appropriate: surface evidence is strong for the oncogenic/stress contexts but not for a baseline surface call.
rationale: HSPA1A (HSP70/HSP72, 641 aa) is canonically a cytoplasmic-nuclear molecular chaperone with no TM domain, no signal peptide, and no GPI anchor. All 8 M1 source databases vote false, consistent with its canonical intracellular role. However, a large body of primary evidence — surface biotinylation of intact viable tumor cells (~15-20% of total cellular Hsp70 detected on the plasma membrane), flow cytometry with the tumor-selective cmHsp70.1 mAb on non-permeabilized cells across colorectal, lung, pancreas, and neuronal carcinomas and leukemia, immunofluorescence in heat-shocked cancer cells, and flow cytometry during immunogenic cell death — establishes conditional outer-leaflet surface localization in transformed and stressed cells. Normal cells (except spermatogenic) are strictly surface-negative. Membrane anchorage is lipid-mediated: Gb3 glycosphingolipid in cholesterol-rich microdomains and heat-shock-induced phosphatidylserine accumulation drive peripheral association with the outer leaflet without a classical TM helix. When surface-localized, the full 641 aa protein — including the C-terminal substrate-binding domain confirmed accessible by cmHsp70.1 mAb epitope mapping — is extracellularly exposed. UniProt additionally annotates HSPA1A as Secreted; circulating eHSP70 is documented, representing a soluble decoy pool. Preclinical extracellular-engagement validation includes cmHsp70.1 antibody binding on live tumor cells and the TKD peptide-NK cell therapeutic approach targeting membrane HSP70. Classification: conditional_surface, outer_leaflet_peripheral, peripheral anchor, exposed_ecd.
